answers.value: Michael M. Larson, P.E., Public Works Director/City Engineer
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form
Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.value:
The Street Department is requesting authorization to purchase a replacement tandem-axle snowplow truck as part of the City’s ongoing fleet replacement program.

Unit #24, a 2007 Sterling tandem-axle plow truck with approximately 148,500 miles and over 10,200 engine hours, is at the end of its useful life for primary snow and ice control. Over the last five winter seasons, this truck has experienced increasingly frequent breakdowns during active snow events, including hydraulic line failures, spreader control issues, and repeated rust-related structural repairs to the dump box and frame components.

In the last two calendar years alone, the City has spent just over $27,000 on major repairs to this unit (transmission work, front suspension and steering components, and electrical system troubleshooting). Our fleet maintenance supervisor has indicated that the chassis frame corrosion is advancing to the point that additional repairs will be more costly and less reliable, and that continued operation as a front-line plow represents an increasing risk for in-service failure. The truck is also no longer compliant with current safety and lighting standards without significant retrofit.

This replacement was identified in the 2025–2029 capital improvement plan (CIP) and scheduled for purchase in 2026. Staff has obtained pricing through the Minnesota state cooperative purchasing program for a new 2026 model tandem-axle plow truck, including stainless steel dump box, front plow, wing plow, underbody scraper, and salt/sand spreader system with pre-wet capability. Utilizing the state contract ensures competitive pricing and compliance with procurement requirements while reducing staff time compared to conducting our own formal bid process.

Timely replacement is important to maintain reliable snow and ice control operations. The City currently operates six primary plow routes. During significant snow events, all six trucks are needed in service to meet our goal of having all primary routes plowed curb-to-curb within 8–10 hours. When Unit #24 is out of service, staff must either double up routes or extend shift lengths, which results in slower response times, increased overtime, and higher risk for operator fatigue. Because we have experienced two significant early-season snowfalls in each of the last three years, staff is requesting that the purchase be authorized now so that the new truck can be ordered for delivery prior to the 2026–2027 winter season.

The replacement truck will be similar in configuration to our existing 2019 and 2021 tandem plows, which will allow for operational consistency, parts interchangeability, and reduced training time for operators. The existing Unit #24 is proposed to be sold at public auction once the new truck is delivered and placed in service. Proceeds from the auction will be returned to the Equipment Fund and used to offset a portion of the purchase cost.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.field_label: Alternatives Options Effects On Others Comments
answers.value:
Alternatives and options considered:

1. **Defer replacement for 1–2 years and continue operating Unit #24**
   - *Pros*: Avoids or delays the upfront capital expenditure in 2026; no immediate impact on the Equipment Fund balance.
   - *Cons*: Based on the maintenance history and condition assessment from our fleet mechanic, we anticipate continued and likely increasing repair costs, potentially in the range of $10,000–$15,000 per year for major components (transmission, hydraulic system, and frame repairs). There is also a significant risk that the truck could suffer a major failure during a snow event, resulting in a plow route being down for an extended period. This would negatively affect our ability to meet level-of-service expectations, particularly on school routes and emergency response corridors. In addition, parts availability for this model year has become more challenging, leading to longer out-of-service times.

2. **Purchase a used plow truck instead of a new truck**
   - *Pros*: Lower upfront cost; depending on the age and condition of the used truck, initial savings could be in the range of $75,000–$100,000 compared to a new unit.
   - *Cons*: The used market for municipal-spec plow trucks is limited, and many available units are themselves near the end of their service life, with unknown maintenance histories and existing corrosion. A used truck would likely have fewer years of reliable service and could lead to higher maintenance and downtime. Warranty coverage would also be limited or nonexistent compared to a new purchase. We would also lose the ability to specify the exact configuration that best fits our routes and our existing inventory of attachments.

3. **Reconfigure existing routes and operate with five primary plow trucks**
   - *Pros*: Avoids immediate replacement purchase; maximizes use of remaining fleet.
   - *Cons*: Staff has modeled this scenario and determined that, to cover all existing routes with only five primary trucks, average route time would increase by approximately 30–40%. This would extend plowing operations well beyond our current 8–10 hour target, particularly during larger snow events, and could result in some residential areas not being fully cleared until the following day. This would likely generate increased complaints from residents and businesses, and school districts have previously expressed concern when their bus routes are not cleared in time for morning runs.

4. **Lease a plow truck instead of purchasing**
   - *Pros*: Spreads costs out over several years and may provide more flexibility for future fleet right-sizing.
   - *Cons*: Staff obtained informal information from two vendors and found that lease rates for heavy-duty plow trucks are relatively high, and most lease structures still require the City to assume full responsibility for maintenance, insurance, and eventual disposal. Over a 10–12 year period, total lease payments would exceed the cost of purchasing the truck outright by an estimated 15–20%, based on preliminary figures. Leasing would also complicate our existing Equipment Fund replacement schedule.

Effects on others:
- **Residents and businesses**: Replacing the truck should help maintain current snow and ice control levels, reducing the likelihood of extended snow-packed conditions, blocked driveways, and accessibility issues following storms.
- **Emergency services**: Police, fire, and ambulance providers rely on timely clearing of key arterial and collector streets. A reliable fleet reduces the chance that critical routes remain partially blocked during peak response times.
- **School districts**: ISD 181 and surrounding districts depend on the City’s ability to clear bus routes prior to morning and afternoon runs. Maintaining a full complement of plow trucks supports consistent school operations and reduces the risk of weather-related delays.
- **City staff**: A reliable new truck will lower operator stress, reduce the need for last-minute schedule changes, and allow more efficient use of overtime.

Comments:
Staff believes that purchasing the replacement truck in 2026, as originally scheduled in the CIP, represents the most cost-effective and operationally sound option. While deferring the purchase could offer short-term budget relief, it would likely increase long-term costs and risk service disruptions during winter weather events. The state cooperative purchasing contract has been reviewed by the Finance Director, and the proposed vendor has supplied similar equipment to the City in the past with satisfactory performance and support.

answers.value: Motion to authorize the purchase of one (1) 2026 model tandem-axle snowplow truck with plow, wing, underbody scraper, and salt/sand spreader package for the Street Department from Northland Truck Equipment, Inc., under the Minnesota state cooperative purchasing contract, in the total amount of $298,450.00 including tax and estimated delivery charges; to approve the associated budget allocation from the Equipment Fund as outlined; and to authorize the Mayor and City Administrator to execute all necessary documents to complete the purchase and dispose of the existing Unit #24 via public auction, with sale proceeds returned to the Equipment Fund.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.field_label: What Is The Total Cost With Tax And Shipping
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form
Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.value:
The total estimated cost for the replacement snowplow truck, including chassis, dump box, plow and wing package, underbody scraper, salt/sand spreader, pre-wet system, dealer setup, and delivery, is $286,200.00 per the state contract pricing. Sales tax and estimated delivery fees add approximately $12,250.00, for a total project cost not to exceed $298,450.00.

This purchase is included in the 2026–2030 Equipment Replacement Plan and was specifically identified in the adopted 2026 Capital Improvement Plan under the Equipment Fund (Fund 620). The 2026 budget includes $300,000.00 for the replacement of one tandem-axle plow truck, based on preliminary estimates prepared in early 2024. The quoted price of $298,450.00 is slightly below the budgeted amount, leaving a small contingency for any minor upfitting or unforeseen delivery-related costs.

Funding will come from the Equipment Fund balance, which is supported by annual internal service charges to the Street Department and other user departments. These internal charges have been set at a level intended to accumulate sufficient funds for scheduled fleet replacements. As of the most recent quarterly report (Q4 2025), the Equipment Fund has an available balance that is adequate to cover this purchase without requiring a transfer from the General Fund.

Once the new truck is delivered and placed in service, the existing 2007 Sterling Unit #24 will be declared surplus and sold through the City’s standard public auction process, likely via an online auction platform that the City has used for prior equipment sales. Based on recent auction results for similar-age plow trucks from other Minnesota municipalities, staff estimates that the City may receive between $18,000.00 and $25,000.00 in sale proceeds, depending on market conditions and the final condition of the truck at the time of sale. Those proceeds will be deposited back into the Equipment Fund and will effectively offset a portion of the replacement cost.

There is no anticipated impact on the City’s tax levy or debt service as a direct result of this purchase, as no bonding or outside financing is proposed. The ongoing operating costs (fuel, routine maintenance, and insurance) for the new truck are expected to be comparable to or slightly lower than the existing unit, due to improved fuel efficiency and reduced major repair needs in the early years of service. Over the expected 12–14 year service life of the truck, staff anticipates that replacing the aging unit now will result in net savings in repair and downtime costs compared to continuing to operate the current truck beyond its planned replacement cycle.
